coredata.dc:title: The tunable synthesis of orange emissive carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ via portable paper sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yu
Zhou, Linan
Li, Zechang
Sun, Peng
Dai, Qijun
Xie, Ruifeng
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Creating a sensitive, accuracy and portable fluorescent probe to monitor Hg2+ with rapid visual convenient process is highly desirable but remains challenging. Herein, orange emissive carbon dots (O-CDs) were synthesized and optimized by solvothermal method using p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) as precursor. The O-CDs emitted an orange emission at 594 nm excited at 480 nm, with QY of 23.25% and a long lifetime of 14.43 μs. The O-CDs exhibited sensitive quenching response towards Hg2+ based on dynamic quenching effect, which facilitated O-CDs utilizing as the fluorescent probe with the limit of detection at 37.6 nM. The O-CDs fulfilled sensing Hg2+ in environmental water with satisfactory recoveries (90.3%−107.8%) and relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.81%, manifested their excellent environmental tolerance. Notably, the O-CDs based test paper was fabricated and served as a portable sensor with a smartphone assistant for detection of Hg2+, which greatly elevated the sensing performance of test paper. This work contributed an innovative approach for the synthesis of O-CDs, as well as a reliable sensitive sensor based on portable test paper for an intelligent readable recognizing system, which could be regarded as promising candidate for real detection of Hg2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Orange emission
Mercury
Detection
Paper sensor
Smartphone

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Test paper-based sensor for the detection of Hg2+
Smartphone application for the detection of Hg2+
Analysis of Hg2+ in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22171060 ) and Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University ( HSDSSCX2023–34 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence spectra of the O-CDs synthesized under (a) different concentrations of p-PDA, various (b) reaction temperature and (c) reaction time. (d) TEM image of O-CDs, inset: HRTEM image (up) and size distribution (down). (e) The XRD pattern of O-CDs. (f) FT-IR of O-CDs and O-CDs+Hg2+. High resolution XPS of (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s and (i) O 1 s.

(a) UV absorption (black), excitation (blue) and emission spectra (red) of O-CDs. (b) The calculated 1931 CIE chromaticity coordinates. (c) Emission of O-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, insert: 3D contour map of excitation, emission wavelength and fluorescent intensity. (d) Fluorescence spectra of O-CDs with various metal ions, inset: the corresponding image under UV lamp. (e) Histogram of fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 of O-CDs mixed with different metal ions. (f) Anti-interference performance of O-CDs towards interfering metal ions. (g) 3D fluorescence spectra of O-CDs mixed with various concentrations of Hg2+. (h) Linear relationship between I0/I and concentration of Hg2+. (i) Corresponding pictures of O-CDs with various concentrations of Hg2+ under UV-lamp.

(a) The sensing selectivity of O-CDs based test paper and smartphone assistant sensing platform. (b) The sensitivity with various concentration of Hg2+ (0–100 μM). (c) The relationship between B/G value with Hg2+ concentrations (0–100 μM). (d) Linear relationship between B/G value with Hg2+ concentrations (0.05–1 μM).

(a) UV–vis absorption of O-CDs and Hg2+ and theoretical and experimental spectra of mixtures of O-CDs and Hg2+. (b) The fluorescence lifetime of fluorescence emission peak at 594 nm of O-CDs with and without Hg2+ (100 μM).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of the O-CDs and their application in sensing Hg2+ via test paper assisted by smartphone.
Determination of O-CDs in real water.

head.ce:title: The tunable synthesis of orange emissive carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ via portable paper sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Biomaterials and Energy Storage Materials, Heilongjiang Province, Key Laboratory for Photonic and Electronic Bandgap Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Normal University, Harbin, 150025, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creating a sensitive, accuracy and portable fluorescent probe to monitor Hg2+ with rapid visual convenient process is highly desirable but remains challenging. Herein, orange emissive carbon dots (O-CDs) were synthesized and optimized by solvothermal method using p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) as precursor. The O-CDs emitted an orange emission at 594 nm excited at 480 nm, with QY of 23.25% and a long lifetime of 14.43 μs. The O-CDs exhibited sensitive quenching response towards Hg2+ based on dynamic quenching effect, which facilitated O-CDs utilizing as the fluorescent probe with the limit of detection at 37.6 nM. The O-CDs fulfilled sensing Hg2+ in environmental water with satisfactory recoveries (90.3%−107.8%) and relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.81%, manifested their excellent environmental tolerance. Notably, the O-CDs based test paper was fabricated and served as a portable sensor with a smartphone assistant for detection of Hg2+, which greatly elevated the sensing performance of test paper. This work contributed an innovative approach for the synthesis of O-CDs, as well as a reliable sensitive sensor based on portable test paper for an intelligent readable recognizing system, which could be regarded as promising candidate for real detection of Hg2+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals pollution is a widely concerned problem because of its increasing and significant negative effects on the human health and environment [1]. Among them, Hg2+ is one of the most toxic heavy metals, which carries great risks to our environment and health [2]. Excessive intake of Hg2+ will stimulate the central nervous system, the function of digestive tract and the kidney, which causes great harm to the human body [3]. What's worse, trace amount of Hg2+ can lead to above disease, thus, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) stipulated the minimum standard in drink water is 2 μg/L (10 nM) [4] and 6 μg/L (30 nM) [5], respectively. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore a simple and effective method for monitoring Hg2+ in real water samples. Currently, a large amount of method have been discovered to detect Hg2+, such as the atomic absorption spectroscopy, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, electrochemical analysis [6], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [7]. However, these methods could not avoid their disadvantages of time-consuming, depending large equipment and complex professional operation process. Therefore, to develop a simple, efficient and convenient method is urgent to need in the real on-site heavy metal detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted extensive attention in recent years in the fields of catalysis, electronic, batteries, sensors, photovoltaic devices etc [8]. due to their low cost, low toxicity, excellent water solubility, unique optical and superior photo-stability [9–11]. Especially in the field of fluorescent sensing, it plays an important role for monitoring heavy metals, bio-molecules, and antibiotics, with high sensitivity and selectivity [12–14]. Fluorescent probe, a crucial application field of CDs, has been developed more successful advantages in detecting heavy metal ions [15]. For instance, Li et al. reported a ratiometric fluorescence sensor fabricated by coordinating 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) sensitized Tb3+ (Tb-DPA) with NH2 and COOH on the surface of the N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for detecting Hg2+ in seafood with limit of detection (LOD) of 37 nM [16]. Omer. et al. constructed a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode probe based on N-doped carbon dots for sensing Fe3+ ions with a lower limit of 0.5 μM [17]. These reports illustrated that the CDs can effectively serve as a fluorescent probe for detection with their advantages of low cost, easy synthesis, non-toxic and visible results.

Noteworthy, the emission wavelength for most reported CDs is usually concentrated on the blue-to-green light region, which greatly limits their further applications in highly sensitive sensing strictly, due to the strong auto-fluorescence at short wavelength emissions by ultraviolet excitation [18]. Red or orange luminescence materials have attracted worldwide attention because of their remarkable sensitivity and little background interference in optosensing [19]. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore the synthetic strategy of orange/red CDs to promote the sensing level and application possibilities [20,21]. Though some reported achieved the synthesis of orange/red fluorescent emissive probe and presented excellent orange/ red emissive sensing behavior with wide sensing scale and little interference and background [22,23], the exploring of approach of fabricating red/orange CDs is still a great challenge. Thus, the orange CDs with good feasibility of detection are eager to design and synthesize for serving in recognizing the heavy metals.

To meet the needs of low-cost, convenient and pollution-free detection on-site, developing miniaturized sensing platforms are urgent needed [24]. However, the portable detection of analysis was still restricted by the complicated operation and unpopularity of laboratory instruments [25]. Therefore, it was necessary to establish comprehensive and manually carried quantitative analysis equipment, such as test paper-based sensors with both sensitivity, selectivity and portability, which have received much attention, but still remained a huge challenge [26,27]. It was difficult to determine the actual content of the measured object directly, because the subtle color changes were not easily recognized by the human naked eye [28]. Simultaneously, the routine monitoring of the water environment requires testing to be conducted on-site at any times, which also poses a test for the accuracy and reliability of rapid in-situ testing [26]. To solve this problem, as high-performance detectors, smartphone based sensor platform was developed and expected to analyze color parameters during real-time detection, and achieved accuracy detection for sensing heavy metal ions for their simple operation, rapid processing speed, and high resolution [29,30].

Inspired this, the orange emissive O-CDs were obtained via an economical and convenient technique by using p-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) as precursors, and exploited to fluorescent probe and test paper for the sensing Hg2+ via assistant of smart phone ( Scheme 1). With high QY (23.25%) and long lifetime (14.43 μs), the orange emissive O-CDs exhibited sensitive quenching response to Hg2+ with variable concentrations systematically, which enables O-CDs fluorescent probe forward Hg2+ quantitatively. The O-CDs achieved detecting Hg2+ in actual environmental water with satisfactory recoveries and low relative standard deviation (RSD). Notably, the fabrication of portable test paper prompted the effective application of O-CDs, and the assistant of smart phone benefited the rapid sensing via intelligent recognition. The high efficiency of utility of O-CDs facilitated the detection of Hg2+ in real on-site environmental test.

All chemical reagents used are analytical grade and without further purification. The reagents p-PDA and Rhodamine B were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ethanol absolute was provided by Tianli Chemical Reagent Company Limited (Tianjin, China). AgNO3, Pb(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2·2 H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6 H2O, MnCl2·4 H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6 H2O, Zn(Ac)2·2 H2O, BaCl2·2 H2O, CaCl2·2 H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4 H2O, Co(NO3)2·7 H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9 H2O, CuSO4·5 H2O and FeSO4·7 H2O were all obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. All the experimental water was deionized water.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained by a HORIBA Jobin Yvon FL-1000 fluorescence spectrometer. The ultraviolet lamp (UV lamp) (WFH-204b) was used to obtain the fluorescent images under UV light. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) measured by HT-7800 at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV was used to explore the morphology and particle size of the O-CDs. The powder X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was recorded on the RigakuDmax2400 diffractometer of CuKa radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was carried out using a JASCOFT/IR-420 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was acquired by an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer and UV–vis absorption spectra was collected by UV-2550 spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Verification of the Hg2+ was performed by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Thermo Scientific iCAP 7400, USA and Agilent 720ES, USA).

Here, O-CDs with orange fluorescence were prepared by solvothermal method using p-PDA and ethanol as precursors. Typically, an oven-dried beaker was charged with p-PDA (0.05 g) and ethanol absolute (10 mL). The reaction mixture was dissolved by stirring at room temperature. After transferring to a high-pressure reactor and heated at 200 °C for 1 h. After cooled to room temperature and collected by centrifugation, the product was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. In the exploration of optimum reaction conditions, various factors such as different reactant ratio, temperature and reaction time were evaluated and compared.

Rhodamine B was selected as the standard reference material to calculate the relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of O-CDs, and the calculation formular is as follows: Yu=Ys*Fu/Fs*Au/As, where Ys is the QY of Rhodamine B; F and A represent the integral area of the fluorescence spectrum, and the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, respectively [31]. The Rhodamine B (dissolved in absolute ethanol) with a QY of 0.89 under the excitation wavelength of 480 nm was selected as the normative reference.

To investigate the selectivity of O-CDs towards Hg2+, O-CDs were diluted 100 times by ethanol as probe, and fluorescence spectra of probe with equal volumes of ions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+ Ag+, Fe3+, NO2 -, NO3 -, SO4 2-, Ac- and Cl-, 100 μM) were recorded and analyzed. For the sake of exploring the sensitivity of O-CDs for Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs probe with various concentrations of Hg2+ (0–500 μM) was tested by comparing their correlation. All experiments were repeated three times to ensure the stability and reliability of the detection.

Tap water (Harbin, China) and Arshaan water (Inner Mongolia, China) were collected (All real water samples were filtered through a syringe (0.22 µm) to remove impurities before using. Then the concentrations of Hg2+ in real water samples were measured by O-CDs.) The feasibility of the O-CDs sensing in real environment were verified by introducing various concentrations of Hg2+ (0.1 μM, 0.3 μM, 0.5 μM) by standard addition method. To further valuate the accuracy, the ICP-OES was employed and the results treated as reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test paper-based sensor for the detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of test paper was achieved by immersing the filter papers in O-CDs solution directly. Firstly, the filter papers cut to the strips with the length of 1 cm and the width of 1 cm, then soaked in the O-CDs solution for 10 min, after taking by tweezers and dried naturally, the fluorescent test paper strips were obtained. The fluorescent test paper was immersed in the sample solution to be tested, dried naturally, then the photo images of test paper were taken under UV lamp by smartphone OPPO K9, and the RGB information was picked by color grab APP and further analyzed to compare and estimate.

Orange emissive O-CDs were successfully fabricated through carbonization of p-PDA (nitrogen sources) via a facile solvothermal method. As reported in previous literature [32], the FL behavior of CDs is the synergistic effect of various factors, including ratio of precursors surface state, surface passivation and carbonaceous core. To achieve highly luminescent CDs, the reaction conditions were optimized by adjusting the ratio of precursors, reaction temperature and reaction time. To obtain the optimized emissive CDs, a large number of experimental parameters including the ratio of two precursors, temperature, and reaction times, were investigated to summarize the best conditions systematically. When the amount of ethanol was fixed 10 mL to change the mass of p-PDA, intense FL could be found ( Fig. 1a), the dramatic increase of the FL intensity of O-CDs may be stemmed from the nitrogen doped into carbon core [33]. Notably, the rising p-PDA caused a significant red shift of emission, which could be attributed to the amino group's enhancement on the surface of O-CDs [34]. Images under UV lamp further verified the FL color evolution from colorless to yellow, then to red and finally to dark brown with the red shift of emission. The marvellous fluorescence property and vivid color evolution proved the imperative role of temperature in the synthesis of O-CDs. On account of evident fluorescence intensity, the 0.05 g p-PDA with 10 mL ethanol were determined as optimal ratio of reagent with optimized temperature of 200 °C (Fig. 1b) by investigating the various conditions. Subsequently, the effect of reaction time was also estimated, and the optimum reaction time was set as 60 min after comparing fluorescence intensity with others (Fig. 1c). The great distinction of fluorescence performance with various reaction time resulted in the high coefficient of difficulties for modulation of synthesis. The less or more reaction time could not provide appropriate reaction time for facilitating the optical properties. The irradiation under UV lamp for 60 min still manifested negligeable change on fluorescence intensity further proved that the excellent stability and anti-bleaching of O-CDs (Fig. S2d), indicated their high capacity of environmental resistance. Based on the above preliminary experimental results, highly luminescent O-CDs were prepared and screened out the optimal detection probe for Hg2+ with the controllable synthesis at appropriate ratio of precursors (0.05 g p-PDA, 10 mL ethanol), 200 °C and 60 min

The morphology and size distribution of O-CDs were investigated by TEM (Fig. 1d). The TEM image and particle size distribution histogram manifested the as-prepared O-CDs were spherical particles, with particle size in the range of 3.4–4.6 nm and average size of 4.0 ± 0.05 nm. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) presented that the crystal plane spacing of O-CDs was 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (0 2 0) facet of graphene (inset in Fig. 1d) [35], suggested successful formation of carbon dots.

The crystallinity and graphitic structure of the O-CDs are also supported by the X–ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, in which the diffraction peaks at 23° (2θ) are consistent with the typically characteristic diffraction of the (0 0 2) plane of graphite–based carbon, manifested the extensive existence of the high conjugated sp2–domain in the O-CDs (Fig. 1e) [36]. For the sake of investigating the surface functional groups of O-CDs, FTIR was acquired. The strong peaks located at 3434, 1638, 1384 and 1125 cm−1 (Fig. 1f) can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H, CO, C-N and C-O, separately [37–40]. The asymmetric stretching vibrations of methylene or methyl groups can be determined at 2852 and 2926 cm−1 [41]. Furthermore, the N-H bending vibration was found at 1564 cm−1 [42]. The FTIR data revealed abundant functional groups such as –OH, –CO dispersed on the surface of O-CDs, which facilitated their capacity of coordination with metals promoted sensing performance.

The XPS was employed to provide further evidence on the elementary composition of O-CDs. The three typical peaks located at 284.6, 398.7 and 530.2 eV (Fig. S1) correspond to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, with the contents of 75.77%, 11.06% and 13.17%, separately (Table S1). The analysis showed that O-CDs was composed with high level doping of nitrogen, which benefited the fluorescence property. The high resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1g) could be grouped into four peaks at 284.80, 286.33, 288.65 and 292.70 eV, which can be assigned to CC/C-C, C-O/C-N, CO and C-H, respectively [43–46]. The N 1 s (Fig. 1h) showed three constituent peaks at 398.55, 399.20 and 400.73 eV, related to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, illustrated the abundant N content [47–49]. In the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1i), the peaks of CO, OC-O and C-O were found at 530.40, 533.28 and 534.60 eV, respectively [50–52]. Additionally, the strong peak at 531.9 eV characterized the binding of N-O, indicated the possibility of coordination sites [53]. The analysis results of XPS were consistent with the result of FTIR, confirming the existence of substantial functional groups of CO, C-O and C-N on the surface of O-CDs.

To evaluate the optical characteristics of O-CDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured. The UV–vis absorption of O-CDs exhibited a typical characteristic absorption peak at 285 nm, which may be respectively assigned to the π-π* transitions of certain sub-fluorophores (e.g., CC, C-N, and C-O) [54]. A relatively wide absorption peak (black line in Fig. 2a) located at 370–600 nm was attributed to n-π * transition of CO bonds, verifying the existence of sp2 structure in O-CDs [55]. A light yellow solution was formed by dissolving O-CDs in ethanol with excellent solubility, and emitted orange fluorescence under UV lamp (inset of Fig. 2a). Moreover, the fluorescent color of O-CDs was further substantiated by CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinate (Fig. 2b), confirmed their orange emission. The maximum emission peak (red line in Fig. 2a) of O-CDs was concentrated on 594 nm excited at 480 nm (blue line in Fig. 2a). The excitation independence was further indicated by the relatively stable emission wavelength with rising excitation from 430 to 530 nm, manifested their relative uniform particle size of O-CDs (Fig. 2c) [56]. The 3D contour spectrum (insert of Fig. 2c) further proved the excellent orange emission of O-CDs. The quantum yield of O-CDs was measured and calculated to be 23.25% by using Rhodamine B as the standard reference.

The stability of O-CDs under diverse circumstance is of great importance to their capacity of actual applications, so the photostability of O-CDs at various conditions including pH, ionic strength and irradiation was estimated. Firstly, the relative fluorescence intensities of O-CDs under various pH (Fig. S2a) manifested fluorescence intensity of O-CDs remained almost no variety with neutral or weakly alkaline solutions, indicated the pH-dependence of O-CDs, which may be related to tunable function of surficial amino groups [57]. The fluorescence intensity also maintained with high concentrations of NaCl solution (0.2–1.0 M) (Fig. S2b), indicating the excellent salt-resistance of O-CDs. With the storage for 6 weeks (Fig. S2c) or irradiated for 3 h by UV lamp (365 nm) (Fig. S2d), the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs remained almost no change, manifested their marvellous resistance to photobleaching. These results demonstrated the excellent photostability performance of O-CDs, which facilitated their multifunctional application in sensing. Considering the physiochemical characteristics of the Hg2+ in aqueous medium in this work, the pH of probe O-CDs was below 5, when mixed with sample equivalently in the detection, the ionic state Hg2+ can be remained dominantly [58].

The diluted 100 times O-CDs by ethanol were determined as probe for subsequent detection research after the evaluation the effect of the ethanol on fluorescence (Fig. S3, supporting information). To discuss the specificity of O-CDs toward Hg2+, O-CDs with equal amounts of ions (Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Fe3+, NO2 -, NO3 -, SO4 2-, Ac- and Cl-, 100 μM) was measured and analyzed. The fluorescence of O-CDs was significantly quenched by Hg2+ ( Fig. 3d), while the others showed negligible fluorescence changes under the same conditions, indicated excellent specificity of O-CDs towards Hg2+. The corresponding images under the UV lamp (inset in Fig. 3d) presented the evident quenching perfection of vibrant orange emission to extermination by Hg2+, further evidenced the excellent selectivity. To clearly assess the selectivity, the ratio of I/I0 for all samples (I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of the absence and presence of ions in O-CDs, respectively) was analyzed and compared. The I/I0 of O-CDs with 100 μM of Hg2+ (Fig. 3e) significantly slumped, which was consistent with the results of fluorescence and corresponding images (Fig. 3d). In comparison, the other samples exhibited almost no change, indicating excellent specificity of O-CDs for Hg2+. To ensure their potential utility in complex environments, the interference of O-CDs towards Hg2+ within relevant ions (Fig. 3f) presented the quenching O-CDs on Hg2+ in the presence of all metal ions was guaranteed without any suspense. Besides, various anions were also involved in the evaluation of selectivity. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. S4a) and photograph (inset in Fig. S4a), relative ratio (I/I0) (Fig. S4b) and anti-interference data (Fig. S4c) demonstrated the negligible signal response of O-CDs to anions. These results confirmed the high selectivity and anti-interference performance of O-CDs for Hg2+.

The sensitivity of O-CDs sensing Hg2+ was further explored by evaluating the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs with various concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0.05 μM to 500 μM) (Fig. 3g). The fluorescence intensity presented the reliable dependence between with concentration of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs at 594 nm gradually descend with the addition of Hg2+. The corresponding fluorescence images under UV lamp (Fig. 3h) also expressed the regular fluorescence color evolution, from the bright vivid red to dim non-blooming with the rising concentration of Hg2+, which was in accordance with the fluorescence spectra results. An exact linear relationship (R2 > 0.999) between I0/I and the concentrations of Hg2+ range from 0.1 to 10.0 μM (Fig. 3i) was obtained, deriving a LOD of 37.6 nM based on 3δ/k (where δ is the standard deviation for the blank solution of eleven measurements and k is the slope of the calibration curve) [59]. The proposed O-CDs based probe exhibited excellent sensitivity, which was comparative even better than the reported method (Table S3), indicated their potential promising application in detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application for the detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Possessing excellent photostability, the fabrication of convenient test paper by extending O-CDs to a cheap filter paper was carried out, and the sensing performance of O-CDs and stability (Fig. S5) were also inherited faithfully. Notably, the assistant of smartphone platform was further explored and established to accomplish the on-site detection promptly, which achieved the facile convenient sensing efficiently. The orange fluorescent paper sensor was obviously changed from orange to blue by exposing to Hg2+ (Fig. 3a), but the other ions reserved original states without obvious change, manifested excellent specificity of test paper towards Hg2+. The O-CDs paper sensor exhibited a color change from orange to blue and gradually dimmed (Fig. 3b) under 365 nm with the Hg2+ concentration increased from 0 to 100 μM, demonstrated good sensing behavior of paper sensor. The rapid intelligent recognition afforded semi-quantitative sensing via analyzing the RGB information (R: red, G: green, B: blue) of fluorescent color of test paper. The RGB values delegated visual quantitative detection via color recognizer App was further proceed to accomplish analysis promptly. The B/G value was chosen as the evaluation criteria after statistical assessment. With the increase of the concentration of Hg2+ (0–100 μM) (Fig. 3c), the regular enhancement of B/G value can be noticed, and an excellent linear relationship between the B/G value and Hg2+ concentration within a range of 0.05–1 μM can be obtained with expression as y = 0.58535x+ 1.53697 (R2 = 0.9919). And the LOD can be determined at 58.8 nM based on 3δ/k (Fig. 3d). In a word, the results demonstrated that the smartphone-assisted fluorescent sensing platform exhibited rapid response, convenient, intelligent and reading out, which reflected their effectively sensing performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Table 1, the result manifested the amount of Hg2+ the average recoveries of samples were between 90.3% and 107.8% with RSD less than 3.81%, verified the excellent environmental tolerance of O-CDs, which benefits sensing application of O-CDs in practical detection. To further verify the accuracy, the stock solution of real water samples with various concentration of Hg2+ were tested by ICP-OES. The results (Table S4) manifested the concentration of stock solution tested by ICP-OES was close to the calibration samples, further identified the excellent accuracy of O-CDs probe.

To understand the quenching mechanism of O-CDs fluorescence produced by Hg2+, a chain of experiments were evaluated. The absorbance of samples ( Fig. 4a) was recorded, as defined in Fig. 4a, the absorption (purple) measured after mixing O-CDs (black) and Hg2+ (red) coincided with the theoretical data (blue) with the absorbance of the two substances separately, which represented no new compounds formed and the electron transfer quenching mechanism was not occurred. As demonstrated, O-CDs possessed multiple surficial groups including -COOH, -NH2, -OH, which can effectively coordinate with Hg2+, causing fluorescence changes even fluorescence quenching [60]. The added Hg2+ coordinated with the surficial groups of O-CDs to induce aggregation of O-CDs, and further resulted in fluorescence quenching. However, there is no significant change in other ions due to their different stability constant, so Hg2+ can be detected with high selectivity using fluorescence [61]. For the better comprehension of the underlying quenching mechanism, the fluorescent lifetime was further analyzed. The fluorescence lifetime of fluorescence emission peak at 594 nm of O-CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ at a concentration of 100 μM was measured and demonstrated in Fig. 4b, which can be fitted with double exponential function:
(1) I(t) = A1 exp(−t/τ1) +A2 exp(−t/τ2)
where A1 and A2 are the fractional contributions to PL emission from two transition channels with corresponding to the decay time or lifetime τ1 and τ2. Double exponential decay lifetime values indicated that electrons have two distinct radiative transition channels [62]. Besides, the average lifetime (τav) was calculated using the following equation:
τav = (A1τ1 2+A2τ2 2)/ (A1τ1+A2τ2)

The average fluorescence lifetime of O-CDs with Hg2+ (8.21 μs) was much shorter than that of O-CDs (14.43 μs), implying that the actual quenching mechanism for orange fluorescence peak at 594 nm was dominated by dynamic quenching, and static type can be excluded [63].

To sum up, a simple, low-cost, and reproducible method for synthesis of orange emissive O-CDs was developed by using p-PDA as precursor, and achieved effective sensing for Hg2+ with portable process. O-CDs systematically exhibited sensitive quenching response to Hg2+ with a reliable linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration with the LOD of 37.6 nM. Notably, an environmentally friendly O-CDs based fluorescent test paper sensor combined with a smartphone platform was established for rapid visual and quantitative detection, and achieved portable, rapid and quantitative sensing of Hg2+ with LOD of 58.5 nM. Satisfied recoveries from real water samples were obtained and confirmed excellent environmental tolerance of O-CDs based probes, indicated O-CDs has great potential for the detection of Hg2+ in the actual environment. These results manifested the O-CDs and derived test paper will be applicable for the accurate detection of Hg2+ in water samples by a rapid, visual, and portable way.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171060) and Graduate Innovation Fund of Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2023–34).

Gao Yu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Dai Qijun: Conceptualization. Xie RuiFeng: Data curation. Zhou Linan: Investigation. Li ZeChang: Investigation. Sun Peng: Investigation. Chai Fang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.111834.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
